html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Twenty-Eight — Complete

Kohelet

KOHELET

Wisdom — Vanity of Vanities

Chapters 1 through 12

Vanity of Vanities All Is Vanity — He Has Put Eternity Into Man's Heart — The Steadfast Love of YHWH Never Ceases — Fear Creator and Keep His Commandments for This Is the Whole Duty of Man

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls — 4QQoh-a

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

Masoretic Text

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

KOHELET — THE ASSEMBLER — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 12

KOHELET — CHAPTER 1 The Words of Kohelet Son of Dawid — Vanity of Vanities All Is Vanity — What Does Man Gain — A Generation Goes and a Generation Comes — There Is No Remembrance of Former Things — I Increased Wisdom and Saw That This Also Is Vanity

1 The words of Kohelet, the son of Dawid, king in Yerushalayim.

2 Vanity of vanities, says Kohelet, vanity of vanities. All is vanity.

3 What does man gain by all the toil at which he toils under the sun?

4 A generation goes, and a generation comes, but the earth remains forever.

5 The sun rises, and the sun goes down, and hastens to its place where it rises.

6 The wind blows to the south and goes around to the north. Around and around goes the wind, and on its circuits the wind returns.

7 All streams run to the sea, but the sea is not full. To the place where the streams flow, there they flow again.

8 All things are full of weariness. A man cannot utter it. The eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing.

9 What has been is what will be, and what has been done is what will be done, and there is nothing new under the sun.

10 Is there a thing of which it is said, See, this is new? It has been already in the ages before us.

11 There is no remembrance of former things, nor will there be any remembrance of things that are to come among those who come after.

12 I, Kohelet, was king over Yisra'EL in Yerushalayim.

13 And I applied my heart to seek and to search out by wisdom all that is done under heaven. It is an unhappy business that Creator has given to the children of man to be busy with.

14 I have seen everything that is done under the sun, and behold, all is vanity and a striving after wind.

15 What is crooked cannot be made straight, and what is lacking cannot be counted.

16 I said in my heart, I have acquired great wisdom, surpassing all who were over Yerushalayim before me, and my heart has had great experience of wisdom and knowledge.

17 And I applied my heart to know wisdom and to know madness and folly. I perceived that this also is but a striving after wind.

18 For in much wisdom is much vexation, and he who increases knowledge increases sorrow.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Dead Sea Scrolls preserve fragments of Kohelet from Cave 4 — 4QQoh-a — the oldest manuscript of Kohelet known, dated to approximately the late second or early first century BCE. The Qumran fragments confirm the canonical text. Kohelet — the Assembler or the Preacher — is the covenant wisdom voice that confronts covenant comfortable covenant theology with the covenant raw covenant honesty of covenant human covenant experience: all is hevel — breath, vapor, vanity.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Kohelet Chapter 1 in full. Verse 2 — vanity of vanities, says Kohelet, vanity of vanities. All is vanity. The covenant superlative covenant construction — hevel havalim — mirrors the Song of Songs' shir hashirim: both covenant books open with the covenant superlative of their covenant central covenant concept. Where Shir HaShirim opens with the covenant highest covenant expression of covenant love, Kohelet opens with the covenant highest covenant expression of covenant impermanence.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 1 in full. Verse 9 — what has been is what will be, and what has been done is what will be done, and there is nothing new under the sun. The covenant cyclical covenant view of covenant history: unlike the covenant linear covenant history of the covenant Nevi'im and the covenant Ketuvim covenant historical books, Kohelet observes the covenant repetitive covenant patterns of covenant human covenant experience and the covenant natural order. The covenant wisdom of Kohelet is the covenant wisdom of covenant pattern-recognition.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — what does man gain by all the toil at which he toils under the sun? The covenant structuring question of the entire covenant book: the covenant Hebrew yitron — gain, profit, advantage — appears ten times in Kohelet and nowhere else in the covenant canon. The covenant commercial covenant vocabulary of covenant profit applied to the covenant totality of covenant human covenant labor is Kohelet's covenant method: subjecting every covenant human covenant activity to the covenant question of what it ultimately produces.

KOHELET — CHAPTER 2 I Said in My Heart Come Now I Will Test You With Pleasure — I Made Great Works — I Gathered Silver and Gold — Whatever My Eyes Desired I Did Not Keep From Them — Then I Considered All My Works — This Also Is Vanity — There Is No Enduring Remembrance

1 I said in my heart, Come now, I will test you with pleasure. Enjoy yourself. But behold, this also was vanity.

2 I said of laughter, It is mad, and of pleasure, What use is it?

3 I searched with my heart how to cheer my body with wine — my heart still guiding me with wisdom — and how to lay hold on folly, till I might see what was good for the children of man to do under heaven during the few days of their life.

4 I made great works. I built houses and planted vineyards for myself.

5 I made myself gardens and parks, and planted in them all kinds of fruit trees.

6 I made myself pools from which to water the forest of growing trees.

7 I bought male and female slaves, and had slaves who were born in my house. I had also great possessions of herds and flocks, more than any who had been before me in Yerushalayim.

8 I also gathered for myself silver and gold and the treasure of kings and provinces. I got singers, both men and women, and many concubines, the delight of the sons of man.

9 So I became great and surpassed all who were before me in Yerushalayim. Also my wisdom remained with me.

10 And whatever my eyes desired I did not keep from them. I kept my heart from no pleasure, for my heart found pleasure in all my toil, and this was my reward for all my toil.

11 Then I considered all that my hands had done and the toil I had expended in doing it, and behold, all was vanity and a striving after wind, and there was nothing to be gained under the sun.

12 So I turned to consider wisdom and madness and folly. For what can the man do who comes after the king? Only what has already been done.

13 Then I saw that there is more gain in wisdom than in folly, as there is more gain in light than in darkness.

14 The wise person has his eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness. And yet I perceived that the same event happens to all of them.

15 Then I said in my heart, What happens to the fool will happen to me also. Why then have I been so very wise? And I said in my heart that this also is vanity.

16 For of the wise as of the fool there is no enduring remembrance, seeing that in the days to come all will have been long forgotten. How the wise dies just like the fool.

17 So I hated life, because what is done under the sun was grievous to me, for all is vanity and a striving after wind.

18 I hated all my toil in which I toil under the sun, seeing that I must leave it to the man who comes after me,

19 And who knows whether he will be wise or a fool? Yet he will be master of all for which I toiled and used my wisdom under the sun. This also is vanity.

20 So I turned about and gave my heart up to despair over all the toil of my labors under the sun,

21 Because sometimes a person who has toiled with wisdom and knowledge and skill must leave everything to be enjoyed by someone who did not toil for it. This also is vanity and a great evil.

22 What has a man from all the toil and striving of heart with which he toils beneath the sun?

23 For all his days are full of sorrow, and his work is a vexation. Even in the night his heart does not rest. This also is vanity.

24 There is nothing better for a person than that he should eat and drink and find enjoyment in his toil. This also, I saw, is from the hand of Creator,

25 For apart from him who can eat or who can have enjoyment?

26 For to the one who pleases him Creator has given wisdom and knowledge and joy, but to the sinner he has given the business of gathering and collecting, only to give to one who pleases Creator. This also is vanity and a striving after wind.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran Kohelet fragments confirm the text. Chapter 2 is Kohelet's covenant experiment with covenant pleasure and covenant achievement — the covenant royal covenant thought experiment in which the covenant most extensive covenant program of covenant human covenant self-gratification is subjected to the covenant question of covenant ultimate covenant value. The covenant conclusion — all was vanity and a striving after wind — verse 11 — is not covenant nihilism but covenant honest covenant assessment.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 24-25 — there is nothing better for a person than that he should eat and drink and find enjoyment in his toil. This also, I saw, is from the hand of Creator. For apart from him who can eat or who can have enjoyment? The covenant first of the covenant seven carpe diem passages in Kohelet — the covenant positive covenant counsel that the covenant book offers as the covenant appropriate covenant human covenant response to the covenant reality of hevel.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 16 — of the wise as of the fool there is no enduring remembrance, seeing that in the days to come all will have been long forgotten. How the wise dies just like the fool. The covenant most challenging covenant observation: the covenant advantage of covenant wisdom over covenant folly — which Kohelet acknowledges — does not extend to covenant death. Death equalizes the covenant wise and the covenant foolish covenant completely. The covenant covenant distinction between wisdom and folly is real but covenant temporary.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 18-19 — I hated all my toil in which I toil under the sun, seeing that I must leave it to the man who comes after me, and who knows whether he will be wise or a fool? The covenant problem of inheritance: the covenant man who accumulated covenant wisdom and covenant wealth through covenant disciplined covenant labor must leave it all to a covenant heir whose covenant character he cannot determine. The covenant covenant irony is acute — the covenant work of a covenant lifetime may be handed to a covenant fool.

KOHELET — CHAPTER 3 For Everything There Is a Season — A Time to Be Born and a Time to Die — What Gain Has the Worker — Creator Has Made Everything Beautiful in Its Time — He Has Put Eternity Into Man's Heart — I Perceived That There Is Nothing Better Than to Be Joyful — The Fate of Man and the Fate of the Beast Is the Same

1 For everything there is a season, and a time for every matter under heaven:

2 A time to be born, and a time to die. A time to plant, and a time to pluck up what is planted.

3 A time to kill, and a time to heal. A time to break down, and a time to build up.

4 A time to weep, and a time to laugh. A time to mourn, and a time to dance.

5 A time to cast away stones, and a time to gather stones together. A time to embrace, and a time to refrain from embracing.

6 A time to seek, and a time to lose. A time to keep, and a time to cast away.

7 A time to tear, and a time to sew. A time to keep silence, and a time to speak.

8 A time to love, and a time to hate. A time for war, and a time for peace.

9 What gain has the worker from his toil?

10 I have seen the business that Creator has given to the children of man to be busy with.

11 He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also put eternity into man's heart, yet so that he cannot find out what Creator has done from the beginning to the end.

12 I perceived that there is nothing better for them than to be joyful and to do good as long as they live.

13 Also that everyone should eat and drink and take pleasure in all his toil — this is Creator's gift to man.

14 I perceived that whatever Creator does endures forever. Nothing can be added to it, nor anything taken from it. Creator has done it, so that people fear before him.

15 That which is, already has been. That which is to be, already has been. And Creator seeks what has been driven away.

16 Moreover, I saw under the sun that in the place of justice, even there was wickedness, and in the place of righteousness, even there was wickedness.

17 I said in my heart, Creator will judge the righteous and the wicked, for there is a time for every matter and for every work.

18 I said in my heart with regard to the children of man that Creator is testing them that they may see that they themselves are but beasts.

19 For what happens to the children of man and what happens to the beasts is the same. As one dies, so dies the other. They all have the same breath, and man has no advantage over the beasts, for all is vanity.

20 All go to one place. All are from the dust, and to dust all return.

21 Who knows whether the spirit of man goes upward and the spirit of the beast goes down into the earth?

22 So I saw that there is nothing better than that a man should rejoice in his work, for that is his lot. Who can bring him to see what will be after him?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran Kohelet fragments confirm the text. Chapter 3 is the covenant most celebrated passage in the covenant book — the covenant poem of appointed times, verses 1-8. The covenant fourteen pairs of covenant opposites — birth and death, plant and pluck, kill and heal, weep and laugh — constitute the covenant comprehensive covenant span of covenant human covenant experience. The covenant poem does not counsel covenant fatalism but covenant recognition: every covenant moment has its covenant appointed covenant character.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 11 — he has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also put eternity into man's heart, yet so that he cannot find out what Creator has done from the beginning to the end. The covenant tension at the center of Kohelet's covenant theology: Creator has placed the covenant awareness of covenant eternity within the covenant human covenant heart — but has simultaneously made it impossible for the covenant human covenant heart to comprehend the full covenant scope of what Creator has done.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 14 — whatever Creator does endures forever. Nothing can be added to it, nor anything taken from it. Creator has done it, so that people fear before him. The covenant permanence of Creator's covenant acts contrasts with the covenant impermanence of all covenant human covenant acts: what Kohelet calls hevel in relation to covenant human covenant endeavor is not hevel in relation to Creator's covenant action. The covenant fear of Creator is the covenant appropriate covenant response to this covenant contrast.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 19-21 — for what happens to the children of man and what happens to the beasts is the same. As one dies, so dies the other. They all have the same breath, and man has no advantage over the beasts, for all is vanity. Who knows whether the spirit of man goes upward? The covenant most challenging anthropological covenant statement in the covenant Ketuvim: Kohelet does not assert covenant afterlife with covenant confidence. The covenant question who knows — mi yodea — is covenant genuine covenant epistemological covenant humility, not covenant denial.

KOHELET — CHAPTER 4 Again I Saw All the Oppressions Done Under the Sun — Better Is a Handful of Quietness — Better Is a Poor and Wise Youth — Two Are Better Than One — A Threefold Cord Is Not Quickly Broken — Better a Poor and Wise Youth Than an Old and Foolish King

1 Again I saw all the oppressions that are done under the sun. And behold, the tears of the oppressed, and they had no one to comfort them. On the side of their oppressors there was power, and there was no one to comfort them.

2 And I thought the dead who are already dead more fortunate than the living who are still alive.

3 But better than both is he who has not yet been and has not seen the evil deeds that are done under the sun.

4 Then I saw that all toil and all skill in work come from a man's envy of his neighbor. This also is vanity and a striving after wind.

5 The fool folds his hands and eats his own flesh.

6 Better is a handful of quietness than two hands full of toil and a striving after wind.

7 Again, I saw vanity under the sun:

8 One person who has no other, either son or brother, yet there is no end to all his toil, and his eyes are never satisfied with riches, so that he never asks, For whom am I toiling and depriving myself of pleasure? This also is vanity and an unhappy business.

9 Two are better than one, because they have a good reward for their toil.

10 For if they fall, one will lift up his fellow. But woe to him who is alone when he falls and has not another to lift him up.

11 Again, if two lie together, they keep warm, but how can one keep warm alone?

12 And though a man might prevail against one who is alone, two will withstand him — a threefold cord is not quickly broken.

13 Better was a poor and wise youth than an old and foolish king who no longer knew how to take advice.

14 For he went from prison to the throne, though in his own kingdom he had been born poor.

15 I saw all the living who move about under the sun, along with that youth who was to stand in the king's place.

16 There was no end of all the people, all of whom he led. Yet those who come later will not rejoice in him. Surely this also is vanity and a striving after wind.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 4 moves from the covenant philosophical to the covenant sociological: Kohelet observes the covenant tears of the covenant oppressed — verse 1 — the covenant motivating force of covenant envy — verse 4 — the covenant tragedy of the covenant solitary worker — verse 8 — and the covenant wisdom of covenant mutual covenant support — verses 9-12. The covenant social covenant observations of Kohelet are covenant empirical covenant theology: he sees what is covenant actually happening under the sun.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 9-12 — two are better than one, because they have a good reward for their toil. For if they fall, one will lift up his fellow. A threefold cord is not quickly broken. The covenant wisdom of covenant communal covenant life over covenant solitary covenant existence: Kohelet's covenant counsel of covenant mutual covenant support is the covenant positive covenant alternative to the covenant tragedy of the covenant isolated covenant worker who never asks for whom he toils.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 1 — behold, the tears of the oppressed, and they had no one to comfort them. On the side of their oppressors there was power, and there was no one to comfort them. The covenant double statement of covenant comfort's covenant absence: the covenant absence of a comforter is mentioned twice in the same verse. The covenant repetition is the covenant literary covenant emphasis on the covenant most painful covenant dimension of covenant oppression — not merely the covenant suffering but the covenant aloneness in the covenant suffering.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 13 — better was a poor and wise youth than an old and foolish king who no longer knew how to take advice. The covenant wisdom of covenant openness to covenant counsel: the covenant king's covenant greatness is measured not by covenant accumulated covenant power but by covenant continued covenant willingness to receive covenant instruction. The covenant king who no longer knows how to take advice has reached the covenant terminal covenant stage of covenant foolishness — covenant power without covenant wisdom, covenant age without covenant growth.

KOHELET — CHAPTER 5 Guard Your Steps When You Go to the House of Creator — Be Not Rash With Your Mouth Before Creator — When You Vow a Vow to Creator — He Who Loves Money Will Not Be Satisfied — As He Came From His Mother's Womb — Everyone to Whom Creator Has Given Wealth This Is Creator's Gift

1 Guard your steps when you go to the house of Creator. To draw near to listen is better than to offer the sacrifice of fools, for they do not know that they are doing evil.

2 Be not rash with your mouth, nor let your heart be hasty to utter a word before Creator, for Creator is in heaven and you are on earth. Therefore let your words be few.

3 For a dream comes with much business, and a fool's voice with many words.

4 When you vow a vow to Creator, do not delay paying it, for he has no pleasure in fools. Pay what you vow.

5 It is better that you should not vow than that you should vow and not pay.

6 Let not your mouth lead you into sin, and do not say before the messenger that it was a mistake. Why should Creator be angry at your voice and destroy the work of your hands?

7 For when dreams increase and words grow many, there is vanity. But Creator is the one you must fear.

8 If you see in a province the oppression of the poor and the violation of justice and righteousness, do not be amazed at the matter, for the high official is watched by a higher, and there are yet higher ones over them.

9 But this is gain for a land in every way: a king committed to cultivated fields.

10 He who loves money will not be satisfied with money, nor he who loves wealth with his income. This also is vanity.

11 When goods increase, they increase who eat them, and what advantage has their owner but to see them with his eyes?

12 Sweet is the sleep of a laborer, whether he eats little or much, but the full stomach of the rich will not let him sleep.

13 There is a grievous evil that I have seen under the sun: riches were kept by their owner to his hurt,

14 And those riches were lost in a bad venture. And he is father of a son, but he has nothing in his hand.

15 As he came from his mother's womb he shall go again, naked as he came, and shall take nothing for his toil that he may carry away in his hand.

16 This also is a grievous evil: just as he came, so shall he go, and what gain is there to him who toils for the wind?

17 Moreover, all his days he eats in darkness in much vexation and sickness and anger.

18 Behold, what I have seen to be good and fitting is to eat and drink and find enjoyment in all the toil with which one toils under the sun the few days of his life that Creator has given him, for this is his lot.

19 Everyone also to whom Creator has given wealth and possessions and power to enjoy them, and to accept his lot and rejoice in his toil — this is the gift of Creator.

20 For he will not much remember the days of his life because Creator keeps him occupied with joy in his heart.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 5 addresses the covenant house of Creator — verse 1 — with the covenant most concentrated covenant counsel on covenant worship in the covenant book: guard your steps, listen rather than sacrifice, let your words be few before Creator who is in heaven. The covenant counsel of covenant few words before Creator is the covenant practical covenant expression of the covenant theological covenant posture established in the covenant poem of appointed times.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 5 in full. Verse 18-19 — behold, what I have seen to be good and fitting is to eat and drink and find enjoyment in all the toil — this is his lot. Everyone also to whom Creator has given wealth and possessions and power to enjoy them — this is the gift of Creator. The covenant second of the covenant carpe diem passages: the covenant ability to enjoy covenant Creator's covenant gifts is itself identified as a covenant gift — not a covenant human covenant achievement but a covenant divine covenant grant.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 5 in full. Verse 2 — Creator is in heaven and you are on earth. Therefore let your words be few. The covenant most concentrated covenant statement of covenant theological covenant humility in Kohelet: the covenant spatial covenant contrast — heaven and earth — establishes the covenant appropriate covenant proportion of covenant human covenant speech before Creator. The covenant many words that covenant fools speak before Creator are the covenant covenant expression of covenant failure to grasp this covenant proportion.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 15 — as he came from his mother's womb he shall go again, naked as he came, and shall take nothing for his toil that he may carry away in his hand. The covenant radical covenant equality of the covenant entry and the covenant exit: the covenant man who accumulated covenant enormous covenant wealth enters and leaves the covenant world with identical covenant material covenant endowment — nothing. The covenant observation is not covenant counsel to covenant abandon covenant wealth but covenant counsel to covenant not be mastered by it.

KOHELET — CHAPTER 6 There Is an Evil That I Have Seen Under the Sun — If a Man Fathers a Hundred Children — A Stillborn Child Is Better Off Than He — All the Toil of Man Is for His Mouth — What Advantage Has the Wise Man Over the Fool — What Is Good for Man

1 There is an evil that I have seen under the sun, and it lies heavy on mankind:

2 A man to whom Creator gives wealth, possessions, and honor, so that he lacks nothing of all that he desires, yet Creator does not give him power to enjoy them, but a stranger enjoys them. This is vanity. It is a grievous evil.

3 If a man fathers a hundred children and lives many years, so that the days of his years are many, but his soul is not satisfied with life's good things, and he also has no burial, I say that a stillborn child is better off than he.

4 For it comes in vanity and goes in darkness, and in darkness its name is covered.

5 Moreover, it has not seen the sun or known anything, yet it finds rest rather than he.

6 Even though he should live a thousand years twice over, yet enjoy no good — do not all go to the one place?

7 All the toil of man is for his mouth, yet his appetite is not satisfied.

8 For what advantage has the wise man over the fool? And what does the poor man have who knows how to conduct himself before the living?

9 Better is the sight of the eyes than the wandering of the appetite. This also is vanity and a striving after wind.

10 Whatever has come to be has already been named, and it is known what man is, and that he is not able to dispute with one stronger than he.

11 The more words, the more vanity, and what is the advantage to man?

12 For who knows what is good for man while he lives the few days of his vain life, which he passes like a shadow? For who can tell man what will be after him under the sun?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 6 is the covenant darkest chapter in Kohelet: the man who has everything but cannot enjoy it — verse 2 — is presented as worse off than the covenant stillborn child — verse 3-5. The covenant ability to enjoy Creator's gifts is more fundamental than the gifts themselves. The covenant chapter ends with the covenant double question that structures the second half of the covenant book: what is good for man? And who can tell man what will be after him?

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 6 in full. Verse 2 — a man to whom Creator gives wealth, possessions, and honor, so that he lacks nothing of all that he desires, yet Creator does not give him power to enjoy them, but a stranger enjoys them. The covenant theological covenant distinction: it is Creator who gives both the covenant material covenant gifts and the covenant capacity to enjoy them. The covenant gifts without the covenant enjoyment-capacity are covenant meaningless. The covenant capacity to enjoy is itself a separate and greater covenant gift.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 6 in full. Verse 12 — for who knows what is good for man while he lives the few days of his vain life, which he passes like a shadow? For who can tell man what will be after him under the sun? The covenant double covenant question that forms the transition point of the covenant book: the first half of Kohelet demonstrated that covenant human covenant achievement is hevel; the second half — Chapters 7-12 — attempts to answer what then is good for man in the face of this covenant reality.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — all the toil of man is for his mouth, yet his appetite is not satisfied. The covenant most compressed covenant statement of the covenant book's covenant central covenant problem: the covenant entire covenant system of covenant human covenant labor is driven by the covenant need to sustain the covenant physical covenant body — and the covenant physical covenant body's covenant appetite is never permanently satisfied. The covenant covenant endless covenant cycle of covenant labor and covenant appetite is the covenant existential covenant trap that Kohelet identifies.

KOHELET — THE SEARCH FOR WHAT IS GOOD — CHAPTERS 7 THROUGH 12

KOHELET — CHAPTER 7 A Good Name Is Better Than Precious Ointment — It Is Better to Go to the House of Mourning — Sorrow Is Better Than Laughter — Do Not Say Why Were the Former Days Better — Wisdom Is Good With an Inheritance — There Is Not a Righteous Man Who Does Not Sin — Creator Has Made Man Upright

1 A good name is better than precious ointment, and the day of death than the day of birth.

2 It is better to go to the house of mourning than to go to the house of feasting, for this is the end of all mankind, and the living will lay it to heart.

3 Sorrow is better than laughter, for by sadness of face the heart is made glad.

4 The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning, but the heart of fools is in the house of mirth.

5 It is better for a man to hear the rebuke of the wise than to hear the song of fools.

6 For as the crackling of thorns under a pot, so is the laughter of the fools. This also is vanity.

7 Surely oppression drives the wise into madness, and a bribe corrupts the heart.

8 Better is the end of a thing than its beginning, and the patient in spirit is better than the proud in spirit.

9 Be not quick in your spirit to become angry, for anger lodges in the heart of fools.

10 Say not, Why were the former days better than these? For it is not from wisdom that you ask this.

11 Wisdom is good with an inheritance, an advantage to those who see the sun.

12 For the protection of wisdom is like the protection of money, and the advantage of knowledge is that wisdom preserves the life of him who has it.

13 Consider the work of Creator: who can make straight what he has made crooked?

14 In the day of prosperity be joyful, and in the day of adversity consider: Creator has made the one as well as the other, so that man may not find out anything that will be after him.

15 In my vain life I have seen everything. There is a righteous man who perishes in his righteousness, and there is a wicked man who prolongs his life in his evildoing.

16 Be not overly righteous, and do not make yourself too wise. Why should you destroy yourself?

17 Be not overly wicked, neither be a fool. Why should you die before your time?

18 It is good that you should take hold of this, and from that withhold not your hand, for the one who fears Creator shall come out from both of them.

19 Wisdom gives strength to the wise man more than ten rulers who are in a city.

20 Surely there is not a righteous man on earth who does good and never sins.

21 Do not take to heart all the things that people say, lest you hear your servant cursing you.

22 Your heart knows that many times you yourself have cursed others.

23 All this I have tested by wisdom. I said, I will be wise, but it was far from me.

24 That which has been is far off, and deep, very deep. Who can find it out?

25 I turned my heart to know and to search out and to seek wisdom and the scheme of things, and to know the wickedness of folly and the foolishness that is madness.

26 And I find something more bitter than death: the woman whose heart is snares and nets, and whose hands are fetters. He who pleases Creator escapes her, but the sinner is taken by her.

27 Behold, this is what I found, says Kohelet, adding one thing to another to find the scheme of things —

28 Which my soul has sought repeatedly, but I have not found. One man among a thousand I found, but a woman among all these I have not found.

29 See, this alone I found, that Creator made man upright, but they have sought out many schemes.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 7 is the covenant longest covenant chapter in Kohelet — twenty-nine verses of covenant concentrated covenant wisdom sayings. The covenant proverbs of verses 1-14 are among the covenant most challenging in the covenant wisdom tradition: sorrow is better than laughter, the house of mourning better than the house of feasting. The covenant wisdom of Kohelet privileges covenant honest covenant engagement with covenant reality over covenant superficial covenant pleasure.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 7 in full. Verse 29 — see, this alone I found, that Creator made man upright, but they have sought out many schemes. The covenant final covenant statement of the covenant chapter is the covenant anthropological covenant conclusion of the covenant entire covenant investigation: the covenant problem is not covenant Creator's covenant design but covenant human covenant deviation from it. The covenant uprightness — yashar — of the covenant original covenant design has been distorted by the covenant human covenant multiplication of covenant schemes.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 7 in full. Verse 13-14 — consider the work of Creator: who can make straight what he has made crooked? In the day of prosperity be joyful, and in the day of adversity consider: Creator has made the one as well as the other. The covenant counsel of covenant theological realism: both covenant prosperity and covenant adversity are Creator's covenant work. The covenant appropriate covenant response to each is covenant distinct — covenant joy in covenant prosperity, covenant considered reflection in covenant adversity.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 16-17 — be not overly righteous, and do not make yourself too wise. Why should you destroy yourself? Be not overly wicked, neither be a fool. Why should you die before your time? The covenant fear of Creator — verse 18 — is the covenant true covenant center.

KOHELET — CHAPTER 8 Who Is Like the Wise Man — Keep the King's Command — Because the Sentence Against an Evil Deed Is Not Executed Speedily — It Will Be Well With Those Who Fear Creator — There Is a Vanity That Takes Place on Earth — No Man Has Power Over the Spirit

1 Who is like the wise man? And who knows the interpretation of a thing? A man's wisdom makes his face shine, and the hardness of his face is changed.

2 I say: Keep the king's command, because of Creator's oath to him.

3 Be not hasty to go from his presence. Do not take your stand in an evil cause, for he does whatever he pleases.

4 For the word of the king is supreme, and who may say to him, What are you doing?

5 Whoever keeps a command will know no evil thing, and the wise heart will know the proper time and the just way.

6 For there is a time and a way for everything, even though man's trouble lies heavy on him.

7 For he does not know what is to be, for who can tell him how it will be?

8 No man has power to retain the spirit, or power over the day of death. There is no discharge from war, nor will wickedness deliver those who are given to it.

9 All this I observed while applying my heart to all that is done under the sun, when man had power over man to his hurt.

10 Then I saw the wicked buried. They used to go in and out of the holy place and were praised in the city where they had done such things. This also is vanity.

11 Because the sentence against an evil deed is not executed speedily, the heart of the children of man is fully set to do evil.

12 Though a sinner does evil a hundred times and prolongs his life, yet I know that it will be well with those who fear Creator, because they fear before him.

13 But it will not be well with the wicked, neither will he prolong his days like a shadow, because he does not fear before Creator.

14 There is a vanity that takes place on earth, that there are righteous people to whom it happens according to the deeds of the wicked, and there are wicked people to whom it happens according to the deeds of the righteous. I said that this also is vanity.

15 And I commend joy, for man has nothing better under the sun but to eat and drink and be joyful, for this will go with him in his toil through the days of his life that Creator has given him under the sun.

16 When I applied my heart to know wisdom, and to see the business that is done on earth, how neither day nor night do one's eyes see sleep,

17 Then I saw all the work of Creator, that man cannot find out the work that is done under the sun. However much man may toil in seeking, he will not find it out. Even though a wise man claims to know, he cannot find it out.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 8 addresses the covenant reality of covenant institutional covenant power — the covenant king's covenant command — and the covenant covenant problem of delayed covenant justice — verse 11. The covenant observation that the covenant delay in executing judgment against covenant evil emboldens covenant further covenant evil is a covenant precise covenant sociological observation that remains permanently accurate. The covenant covenant response — it will be well with those who fear Creator — verse 12 — is the covenant anchor.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 8 in full. Verse 17 — man cannot find out the work that is done under the sun. However much man may toil in seeking, he will not find it out. Even though a wise man claims to know, he cannot find it out. The covenant epistemological covenant conclusion of the covenant chapter: the covenant limitation of covenant human covenant knowledge is absolute in relation to the covenant total work of Creator. The covenant wise man who claims covenant comprehensive covenant knowledge is the covenant most covenant deceived.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 8 in full. Verse 14 — there are righteous people to whom it happens according to the deeds of the wicked, and there are wicked people to whom it happens according to the deeds of the righteous. The covenant same covenant theodicy problem that Iyov raised — now stated by Kohelet as a covenant calm covenant observation rather than a covenant anguished covenant protest. The two covenant books approach the covenant same covenant reality from covenant different covenant emotional covenant registers.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — no man has power to retain the spirit, or power over the day of death. There is no discharge from war, nor will wickedness deliver those who are given to it. The covenant four covenant observations about covenant human covenant powerlessness: over the covenant spirit, over the covenant day of death, over the covenant discharge from covenant war, and over covenant deliverance from covenant wickedness's covenant consequences. The covenant four areas of covenant absolute covenant human covenant powerlessness are the covenant boundaries of covenant human covenant existence.

KOHELET — CHAPTER 9 All This I Laid to Heart — The Same Event Happens to All — The Living Know That They Will Die — There Is No Work in Sheol — Go Eat Your Bread With Joy — Whatever Your Hand Finds to Do Do It With Your Might — The Race Is Not to the Swift

1 But all this I laid to heart, examining it all, how the righteous and the wise and their deeds are in the hand of Creator. Whether it is love or hate, man does not know. Both are before him.

2 It is the same for all, since the same event happens to the righteous and the wicked, to the good and the evil, to the clean and the unclean, to him who sacrifices and him who does not sacrifice. As the good one is, so is the sinner, and he who swears is as he who shuns an oath.

3 This is an evil in all that is done under the sun, that the same event happens to all. Also, the hearts of the children of man are full of evil, and madness is in their hearts while they live, and after that they go to the dead.

4 But he who is joined with all the living has hope, for a living dog is better than a dead lion.

5 For the living know that they will die, but the dead know nothing, and they have no more reward, for the memory of them is forgotten.

6 Their love and their hate and their envy have already perished, and forever they have no more share in all that is done under the sun.

7 Go, eat your bread with joy, and drink your wine with a merry heart, for Creator has already approved what you do.

8 Let your garments be always white. Let not oil be lacking on your head.

9 Enjoy life with the wife whom you love, all the days of your vain life that he has given you under the sun, because that is your portion in life and in your toil at which you toil under the sun.

10 Whatever your hand finds to do, do it with your might, for there is no work or thought or knowledge or wisdom in Sheol, to which you are going.

11 Again I saw that under the sun the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong, nor bread to the wise, nor riches to the intelligent, nor favor to those with knowledge, but time and chance happen to them all.

12 For man does not know his time. Like fish that are taken in an evil net, and like birds that are caught in a snare, so the children of man are snared at an evil time, when it suddenly falls upon them.

13 I have also seen this example of wisdom under the sun, and it seemed great to me.

14 There was a little city with few men in it, and a great king came against it and besieged it, building great siegeworks against it.

15 But there was found in it a poor, wise man, and he by his wisdom delivered the city. Yet no one remembered that poor man.

16 But I say that wisdom is better than might, though the poor man's wisdom is despised and his words are not heard.

17 The words of the wise heard in quiet are better than the shouting of a ruler among fools.

18 Wisdom is better than weapons of war, but one sinner destroys much good.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 9 contains the covenant most concentrated covenant counsel for covenant full covenant engagement with covenant present covenant life: eat your bread with joy — verse 7, enjoy life with the wife whom you love — verse 9, whatever your hand finds to do, do it with your might — verse 10. The covenant urgency is grounded in the covenant reality of Sheol — there is no work or thought or knowledge or wisdom in Sheol, to which you are going.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 9 in full. Verse 11 — the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong, nor bread to the wise, nor riches to the intelligent, nor favor to those with knowledge, but time and chance happen to them all. The covenant most celebrated covenant observation about the covenant relationship between covenant human covenant capacity and covenant actual covenant outcome: the covenant alignment of covenant ability and covenant result cannot be guaranteed. Time and chance — et and pega — operate across all covenant categories.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 9 in full. Verse 14-15 — there was a little city with few men, and a great king came against it — but there was found in it a poor, wise man, and he by his wisdom delivered the city. Yet no one remembered that poor man. The covenant parable of the covenant forgotten covenant deliverer: covenant wisdom that saved a covenant city produced no covenant lasting covenant memorial for the covenant wise man who deployed it. The covenant covenant reward of covenant wisdom is not covenant guaranteed covenant recognition.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — the righteous and the wise and their deeds are in the hand of Creator. Whether it is love or hate, man does not know. Both are before him. The covenant theological covenant foundation of the covenant chapter: the covenant ultimate covenant disposition of the covenant righteous and the covenant wise — whether they will receive covenant divine covenant love or covenant divine covenant opposition — is not covenant knowable by covenant human covenant beings. This covenant epistemological covenant limitation is not covenant paralysis but covenant freedom to trust Creator.

KOHELET — CHAPTER 10 Dead Flies Make the Perfumer's Ointment Give Off a Stench — The Heart of the Wise Inclines to the Right — Even in Your Bedroom Do Not Curse the King — Cast Your Bread Upon the Waters — He Who Watches the Wind Will Not Sow

1 Dead flies make the perfumer's ointment give off a stench. So a little folly outweighs wisdom and honor.

2 A wise man's heart inclines him to the right, but a fool's heart to the left.

3 Even when the fool walks on the road, he lacks sense, and he says to everyone that he is a fool.

4 If the anger of the ruler rises against you, do not leave your place, for calmness will lay great offenses to rest.

5 There is an evil that I have seen under the sun, as it were an error proceeding from the ruler:

6 Folly is set in many high places, and the rich sit in a low place.

7 I have seen slaves on horses, and princes walking on the ground like slaves.

8 He who digs a pit will fall into it, and a serpent will bite him who breaks through a wall.

9 He who quarries stones is hurt by them, and he who splits logs is endangered by them.

10 If the iron is blunt, and one does not sharpen the edge, he must use more strength, but wisdom helps one to succeed.

11 If the serpent bites before it is charmed, there is no advantage to the charmer.

12 The words of a wise man's mouth win him favor, but the lips of a fool consume him.

13 The beginning of the words of his mouth is foolishness, and the end of his talk is evil madness.

14 A fool multiplies words, though no man knows what is to be, and who can tell him what will be after him?

15 The toil of a fool wearies him, for he does not know the way to the city.

16 Woe to you, O land, when your king is a child, and your princes feast in the morning.

17 Happy are you, O land, when your king is the son of the nobility, and your princes feast at the proper time, for strength, and not for drunkenness.

18 Through sloth the roof sinks in, and through indolence the house leaks.

19 Bread is made for laughter, and wine gladdens life, and money answers everything.

20 Even in your thoughts, do not curse the king, nor in your bedroom curse the rich, for a bird of the air will carry your voice, or some winged creature will tell the matter.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 10 is a covenant collection of covenant wisdom sayings about the covenant relationship between covenant wisdom and covenant folly in covenant practical covenant life — particularly in covenant institutional and covenant political covenant contexts. The covenant dead flies — verse 1 — is the covenant most memorable covenant image: a small quantity of covenant folly can ruin a large quantity of covenant wisdom, just as dead flies ruin a whole batch of precious ointment.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 10 in full. Verse 20 — even in your thoughts, do not curse the king, nor in your bedroom curse the rich, for a bird of the air will carry your voice, or some winged creature will tell the matter. The covenant counsel of covenant consistent covenant integrity: covenant private covenant speech and covenant public covenant speech must covenant align. The covenant famous covenant proverbial covenant warning — a covenant bird of the air will carry your voice — grounds the covenant counsel of covenant private covenant integrity in the covenant practical reality of covenant information covenant disclosure.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 10 in full. Verse 16-17 — woe to you, O land, when your king is a child, and your princes feast in the morning. Happy are you, O land, when your king is the son of the nobility, and your princes feast at the proper time, for strength, and not for drunkenness. The covenant wisdom of covenant appropriate covenant timing: the covenant leaders who eat at covenant proper covenant time for covenant strength represent the covenant covenant between covenant privilege and covenant responsibility.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — dead flies make the perfumer's ointment give off a stench. So a little folly outweighs wisdom and honor. The covenant asymmetry between covenant wisdom and covenant folly: it requires significantly more covenant effort to build covenant wisdom and covenant honor than to destroy them. The covenant small quantity of covenant folly that ruins the covenant perfumer's covenant whole batch of covenant ointment is the covenant image of how covenant disproportionately damaging a covenant small covenant foolishness can be in the context of a covenant lifetime of covenant covenant careful covenant wisdom.

KOHELET — CHAPTER 11 Cast Your Bread Upon the Waters — Give a Portion to Seven or Even to Eight — He Who Watches the Wind Will Not Sow — As You Do Not Know the Work of Creator Who Makes Everything — Rejoice O Young Man in Your Youth — Remove Vexation From Your Heart

1 Cast your bread upon the waters, for you will find it after many days.

2 Give a portion to seven, or even to eight, for you know not what disaster may happen on earth.

3 If the clouds are full of rain, they empty themselves on the earth, and if a tree falls to the south or to the north, in the place where the tree falls, there it will lie.

4 He who watches the wind will not sow, and he who looks at the clouds will not reap.

5 As you do not know the way the spirit comes to the bones in the womb of a woman with child, so you do not know the work of Creator who makes everything.

6 In the morning sow your seed, and at evening withhold not your hand, for you do not know which will prosper, this or that, or whether both alike will be good.

7 Light is sweet, and it is pleasant for the eyes to see the sun.

8 So if a person lives many years, let him rejoice in them all. But let him remember that the days of darkness will be many. All that comes is vanity.

9 Rejoice, O young man, in your youth, and let your heart cheer you in the days of your youth. Walk in the ways of your heart and the sight of your eyes. But know that for all these things Creator will bring you into judgment.

10 Remove vexation from your heart, and put away pain from your body, for youth and the dawn of life are vanity.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 11 is the covenant call to covenant bold covenant action in the face of covenant irreducible covenant uncertainty: cast your bread upon the waters — verse 1, give a portion to seven or even to eight — verse 2, sow in the morning and withhold not your hand at evening — verse 6. The covenant uncertainty of covenant outcomes is not a covenant reason for covenant paralysis but a covenant reason for covenant generous and covenant persistent covenant action.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 11 in full. Verse 5 — as you do not know the way the spirit comes to the bones in the womb of a woman with child, so you do not know the work of Creator who makes everything. The covenant analogy of the covenant mystery of covenant life in the covenant womb to the covenant mystery of Creator's covenant total work: both are covenant inaccessible to covenant human covenant knowledge. The covenant acknowledgment of covenant mystery is the covenant foundation of the covenant chapter's covenant counsel of covenant bold covenant generosity.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 11 in full. Verse 9 — rejoice, O young man, in your youth, and let your heart cheer you in the days of your youth. Walk in the ways of your heart and the sight of your eyes. But know that for all these things Creator will bring you into judgment. The covenant most balanced covenant counsel in the covenant book: the covenant invitation to covenant full covenant joy in covenant youth is covenant simultaneously qualified by the covenant reminder of covenant divine covenant accountability. The covenant joy is real; the covenant judgment is real; both must be held.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — he who watches the wind will not sow, and he who looks at the clouds will not reap. The covenant counsel against covenant paralysis through covenant excessive covenant caution: the covenant farmer who waits for perfect covenant conditions before covenant sowing will never sow. The covenant uncertainty of covenant outcomes cannot be eliminated by covenant observation — it can only be covenant accepted and covenant acted through. The covenant wisdom of Kohelet at its covenant most practical: covenant act in covenant uncertainty rather than covenant refuse to covenant act until certainty arrives.

KOHELET — CHAPTER 12 Remember Your Creator in the Days of Your Youth — Before the Evil Days Come — The Dust Returns to the Earth — The Spirit Returns to Creator Who Gave It — Vanity of Vanities All Is Vanity — Fear Creator and Keep His Commandments for This Is the Whole Duty of Man

1 Remember your Creator in the days of your youth, before the evil days come and the years draw near of which you will say, I have no pleasure in them.

2 Before the sun and the light and the moon and the stars are darkened and the clouds return after the rain,

3 In the day when the keepers of the house tremble, and the strong men are bent, and the grinders cease because they are few, and those who look through the windows are dimmed,

4 And the doors on the street are shut — when the sound of the grinding is low, and one rises up at the sound of a bird, and all the daughters of song are brought low —

5 They are afraid also of what is high, and terrors are in the way. The almond tree blossoms, the grasshopper drags itself along, and desire fails, because man is going to his eternal home, and the mourners go about the streets —

6 Before the silver cord is snapped, or the golden bowl is broken, or the pitcher is shattered at the fountain, or the wheel broken at the cistern,

7 And the dust returns to the earth as it was, and the spirit returns to Creator who gave it.

8 Vanity of vanities, says Kohelet. All is vanity.

9 Besides being wise, Kohelet also taught the people knowledge, weighing and studying and arranging many proverbs with great care.

10 Kohelet sought to find words of delight, and uprightly he wrote words of truth.

11 The words of the wise are like goads, and like nails firmly fixed are the collected sayings. They are given by one Shepherd.

12 My son, beware of anything beyond these. Of making many books there is no end, and much study is a weariness of the flesh.

13 The end of the matter. All has been heard. Fear Creator and keep his commandments, for this is the whole duty of man.

14 For Creator will bring every deed into judgment, with every secret thing, whether good or evil.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran Kohelet fragments confirm the text of Chapter 12. The covenant closing chapter of Kohelet contains the covenant most magnificent covenant poem about covenant aging and covenant death in the covenant canon — verses 1-7 — an extended covenant allegory in which every covenant image represents a covenant faculty of the covenant aging covenant human body declining toward the covenant dust from which it came and the covenant spirit returning to Creator who gave it. The covenant cycle is complete.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 12 in full. Verse 13-14 — the end of the matter. All has been heard. Fear Creator and keep his commandments, for this is the whole duty of man. For Creator will bring every deed into judgment, with every secret thing, whether good or evil. The covenant final covenant statement of the entire covenant book: after the covenant entire covenant investigation of hevel and the covenant entire covenant search for what is good for man, the covenant conclusion is the covenant same as the covenant beginning of covenant wisdom — the fear of Creator — and its covenant concrete covenant expression — the keeping of his covenant commandments.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 12 in full. Verse 7 — the dust returns to the earth as it was, and the spirit returns to Creator who gave it. The covenant theological covenant anthropology of the covenant closing covenant poem: the covenant dust-dimension of the covenant human covenant person returns to the covenant earth from which it came — Bereshit 3:19 — and the covenant spirit-dimension returns to Creator who gave it. The covenant covenant death of Kohelet is the covenant return of what was lent to its covenant original source.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — the words of the wise are like goads, and like nails firmly fixed are the collected sayings. They are given by one Shepherd. The covenant most concentrated covenant statement about the covenant nature and covenant source of covenant wisdom literature: the covenant words of the covenant wise are covenant simultaneously covenant painful covenant stimulants — like goads that drive cattle forward — and covenant permanently covenant anchored truth — like nails firmly fixed. Both covenant functions are necessary. And they are given by one Shepherd — the covenant image of YHWH as the covenant single covenant source of all covenant authentic covenant wisdom.

KOHELET — COMPLETE
